Narrator: Title: 2 B R 0 2 B — A narrated adaptation from the story by Kurt Vonnegut.

Narrator: Everything, by official account, was wonderful.

Narrator: There were no prisons. No slums. No wars. No poverty. No incurable diseases. No old age, at least not in the old sense. People no longer withered away by the calendar. They simply continued, bright-eyed and useful, unless an accident found them—or unless they volunteered for death.

Narrator: In the United States, the population had been fixed at forty million people. Not forty million and one. Not forty million minus one for long. Every arrival had to be balanced by a departure. That was the rule that kept the clean, peaceful world from bursting.

Narrator: On a clear morning at the Chicago Lying-in Hospital, Edward K. Wehling, Junior, sat alone in a waiting room while his wife gave birth. He was fifty-six years old, young by the standards of a nation whose average citizen had seen more than a century. These were to be his first children.

Narrator: The doctors already knew there would be three.

Narrator: Triplets.

Narrator: Wehling sat hunched forward, his head in his hands, as if he were trying to make himself smaller than the chair. His clothes were rumpled. His face was drained. In the disorder of the waiting room, he almost disappeared.

Narrator: The room itself looked unsettled. Chairs had been pushed from the walls. Ashtrays stood in odd places. The floor was covered with paint-spattered cloths. The hospital was redecorating the room as a memorial to a man who had recently volunteered to die.

Narrator: On a stepladder, an old painter worked at a mural he despised.

Narrator: He was nearly two hundred years old, though he looked, at most, like a man in early middle age. He had begun to age before the cure had been discovered, and so a faint trace of mortality remained on him. It gave him a sardonic look, as if life had played a joke and he had not forgotten the punch line.

Narrator: The mural showed a perfect garden.

Narrator: In it, doctors and nurses in white planted and watered and tended young life. People in purple uniforms trimmed, clipped, removed, and carried away what was old, sick, or excessive. Every plant had just enough soil, light, water, and space. Nothing crowded anything else. Nothing wasted anything else. It was orderly beyond nature, neat beyond beauty.

Narrator: The mural’s official title was “The Happy Garden of Life.”

Narrator: The painter dabbed at it with the resignation of a man paid to lie in colors.

Narrator: An orderly came along the corridor, softly singing a popular little tune about visiting a girl in purple if romance failed, and leaving the world so some sweet baby could take his place. He paused at the door and admired the painting.

Narrator: He said it looked so real he could imagine standing inside it.

Narrator: The painter, without warmth, asked what made him so sure he wasn’t already in it.

Narrator: The orderly laughed, or tried to. He commented that it must be nice to make pictures that actually resembled things.

Narrator: The painter looked down at the filthy drop cloths beneath him and said that if anyone wanted an honest picture of life, they ought to frame one of those.

Narrator: The orderly called him a gloomy old bird.

Narrator: The painter asked whether gloom was illegal now.

Narrator: The orderly shrugged and suggested that if the painter disliked the world so much, he knew the number to call. He recited it in the common, joking way: two B R naught two B.

Narrator: 2 B R 0 2 B.

Narrator: It was a telephone number everyone knew. Some called the place the Automat, or the Cannery, or Easy-Go, or Weep-No-More. Some used crueler names. Officially, it belonged to the Federal Bureau of Termination, the city’s carefully managed gas chambers.

Narrator: The phrase behind the number was old, older than population control, older than the cheerful purple uniforms. To be, or not to be.

Narrator: The painter said that when he chose to go, he would not do it in one of those official rooms.

Narrator: The orderly teased him for being a do-it-yourself man, and warned him to think of the cleanup.

Narrator: The painter answered that the world could use more mess, not less.

Narrator: The orderly laughed again and went on his way.

Narrator: From his chair, Edward Wehling mumbled something. He did not raise his head. Then he sank back into silence.

Narrator: A woman in purple entered the waiting room with a hard, efficient click of high heels. Her shoes, stockings, trench coat, handbag, and little overseas cap were all the same official purple—a color the painter privately thought of as grapes on Judgment Day.

Narrator: On her bag was the emblem of the Service Division of the Federal Bureau of Termination: an eagle perched on a turnstile.

Narrator: Her name was Leora Duncan. Like many hostesses from the termination service, she had developed a surprising mustache, though she seemed otherwise quite ordinary, even shy.

Narrator: She asked whether this was where she was supposed to report for her portrait.

Narrator: The painter looked down from his ladder and said that depended on her business. She wasn’t there to have a baby, was she?

Narrator: She explained that she had been told to pose for the mural.

Narrator: The painter found her name on his list. Yes, Leora Duncan was to be immortalized in “The Happy Garden of Life.” He asked which faceless figure she preferred.

Narrator: She studied the mural and admitted she knew nothing about art. The figures looked much the same to her.

Narrator: The painter first suggested a woman carrying dead stalks to be burned, but Leora objected gently. That, she said, looked more like disposal work. She was in service.

Narrator: The painter clapped his hands in mock admiration. She knew more about art than she realized. Of course. A hostess would not be hauling refuse. She would be pruning.

Narrator: He pointed to a purple-uniformed woman sawing a dead branch from an apple tree. That figure stood near a powerful white-clad man whose face had already been painted.

Narrator: Leora blushed. That would put her right next to Dr. Benjamin Hitz.

Narrator: Dr. Hitz, the hospital’s chief obstetrician, was a legend: tall, handsome, white-haired, more than two hundred years old, and almost worshiped by those who believed in the system he helped build. He had been instrumental in establishing Chicago’s first gas chamber.

Narrator: Leora regarded his portrait with reverence. To be painted beside him, she said, would be an honor.

Narrator: The painter agreed to place her there for all time, cutting away a limb.

Narrator: That, Leora admitted, was rather like what she did.

Narrator: What she did, in plain terms, was make people comfortable while they died.

Narrator: While Leora posed, Dr. Benjamin Hitz himself burst into the room. He was enormous—seven feet tall—and he seemed to carry with him a climate of success, confidence, and booming goodwill.

Narrator: He greeted Leora with hearty surprise and joked that she was in the wrong part of the hospital. This, he said, was where people came in, not where they went out.

Narrator: Leora told him they were going to appear together in the mural.

Narrator: Dr. Hitz declared himself honored. Without women like her, he said, the beautiful modern world would not be possible.

Narrator: Then he turned toward the delivery rooms and announced the news: triplets had just been born.

Narrator: Leora’s reaction was not merely delight. It was calculation, immediate and professional. Under the law, no newborn child could remain alive unless a volunteer agreed to die in that child’s place. One birth required one death. Triplets required three.

Narrator: She asked whether the parents had three volunteers.

Narrator: Dr. Hitz said that, as far as he knew, they had one and were trying to find two more.

Narrator: Leora said she did not think they had succeeded. No group of three appointments had been scheduled at the Bureau that day.

Narrator: From the chair, the waiting father lifted his head. His eyes were red. His face was slack with exhaustion and dread.

Narrator: “Wehling,” he said. Edward K. Wehling, Junior. The happy father-to-be.

Narrator: He raised a hand as though answering roll call and said, hollowly, “Present.”

Narrator: Dr. Hitz had not even noticed him sitting there.

Narrator: Wehling called himself the invisible man.

Narrator: Dr. Hitz told him that the babies were born, all three healthy, and their mother was well.

Narrator: Wehling answered with a flat little “hooray” that contained no joy at all.

Narrator: Dr. Hitz asked why he did not sound happy.

Narrator: Wehling said any man in his position would surely be delighted. All he had to do was decide which one of his three newborn children should live, deliver his maternal grandfather to the termination center, collect a receipt, and return.

Narrator: Dr. Hitz’s warmth cooled. He towered over Wehling and asked whether he believed in population control.

Narrator: Wehling said he thought it was wonderful, though his voice was tight enough to snap.

Narrator: Dr. Hitz reminded him of the old nightmare: a planet swollen toward twenty billion people, then forty, then eighty, then more. He described humanity packed across the earth like the tiny pulpy beads of a blackberry. Without control, he said, there would have been no room, no water, no food—only crowding, hunger, and panic.

Narrator: In the year 2000, Dr. Hitz said, the world had been nearly finished. People had demanded the right to have children and the right to live forever, all on a planet that could not stretch to fit them.

Narrator: Wehling stared at the wall.

Narrator: Quietly, he said he wanted the children. All three.

Narrator: Dr. Hitz softened. Of course he did. That was only human.

Narrator: Wehling added that he did not want his grandfather to die, either.

Narrator: Dr. Hitz replied that no one enjoyed escorting a close relative to the termination studios.

Narrator: Leora gently objected when he used one of the slang names for the place. Such names, she said, gave people the wrong idea.

Narrator: Dr. Hitz corrected himself and used the official title: Ethical Suicide Studios.

Narrator: Leora said that sounded much nicer.

Narrator: Dr. Hitz turned back to Wehling. Whichever baby he chose to keep, the doctor said, would grow up on a clean, spacious, prosperous planet. A planet like the garden on the wall. Two centuries earlier, the world had seemed doomed. Now, thanks to population control, peace and plenty could extend for ages.

Narrator: He smiled with radiant certainty.

Narrator: Then the smile vanished.

Narrator: Edward Wehling had drawn a revolver.

Narrator: He shot Dr. Hitz first.

Narrator: As the great man fell, Wehling said there was room now for one—one very large one.

Narrator: Then he shot Leora Duncan.

Narrator: As she collapsed, he told her it was only death. Now there was room for two.

Narrator: Finally, Edward K. Wehling, Junior, turned the gun on himself.

Narrator: With that, there was room for all three of his children.

Narrator: No one rushed in. No alarm sounded. No footsteps thundered down the corridor. If anyone heard the shots, no one came.

Narrator: The painter remained on his stepladder, looking down at the bodies in the half-decorated waiting room. Around him, the cheerful garden glowed in unfinished colors: white figures nurturing, purple figures pruning, all of it so clean, so reasonable, so terrible.

Narrator: He thought about the old puzzle that had never been solved by science, only managed by rules. Life demanded to be born. Once born, it demanded to grow, to multiply, to cling on as long as possible. And all of this had to happen on one small planet that must somehow last forever.

Narrator: The answers he could imagine were darker than the Bureau’s chambers. War. Disease. Starvation. Collapse.

Narrator: He knew he would not paint again.

Narrator: He let his brush fall. It landed softly on the stained drop cloths below.

Narrator: Then he climbed down from the ladder and took Wehling’s pistol. For a moment he meant to use it on himself.

Narrator: But he could not.

Narrator: In the corner of the waiting room stood a telephone booth.

Narrator: The painter walked to it, shut himself inside, and dialed the number everyone knew.

Narrator: 2 B R 0 2 B.

Narrator: A hostess answered in a warm, practiced voice: Federal Bureau of Termination.

Narrator: The painter asked how soon he could have an appointment.

Narrator: The hostess said they could likely fit him in late that afternoon—perhaps earlier, if there was a cancellation.

Narrator: The painter accepted. Carefully, he gave his name and spelled it out.

Narrator: The hostess thanked him. His city thanked him, she said. His country thanked him. His planet thanked him. And most of all, future generations thanked him.

Narrator: And in the waiting room of the Chicago Lying-in Hospital, beneath the unfinished mural of the Happy Garden of Life, the painter listened until the line went quiet.
